reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: "When to use" and "When NOT to use" are correctly placed as peer H2 sections, not nested — avoids the TOC-breaking antipattern.
Realistic, directly copyable examples (SES email identity, SSM parameter, CloudWatch log group with RetentionInDays) — not toy examples.
The FeatureComparisonTable comparing Raw CloudFormation vs CDK Constructs vs Overrides is exactly what a developer needs before picking an approach. High citation potential.

Comprehensive 9-pair FAQ covering all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (connectTo), AWS-service questions (500-resource limit, DeletionPolicy), and comparison questions (raw CF vs CDK, raw CF vs overrides).
Intrinsic functions mapping table (CloudFormation syntax → TypeScript object form) is a practical reference that readers will return to. Tables are cited 2–4× more than equivalent prose.
All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> — no YAML blocks, no fenced TypeScript violations, no StacktapeConfig import pattern.
The "Avoiding naming conflicts" section is grounded in the source JSDoc ("Use stacktape stack-info --detailed") and surfaced before it becomes a surprise failure in production.
Opening paragraph is self-contained and 40–75 words — ready for AI engine extraction.
problems: <ApiReference definitionName="ConfigRoot" /> is potentially too broad. The root config type (StacktapeConfig / ConfigRoot) has dozens of top-level properties; showing the entire root schema on a page focused only on cloudformationResources may overwhelm readers who just want to explore the CloudformationResource shape. If the schema exposes a dedicated CloudformationResource definition, that is the correct definitionName for this page.

The second code example returns resources: {} (an empty object). While technically valid per the source interface, no prose nearby explains that resources may be empty when the stack only contains cloudformationResources. A beginner will reasonably assume resources is required to be non-empty and get confused.

The Tip and Warning callouts appear back-to-back in the "Avoiding naming conflicts" section, covering adjacent (not distinct) concerns. Two callouts in sequence creates visual noise without proportional information gain; the Warning in particular restates a caveat already implied by the Tip.
mandatoryFixes: Verify that "ConfigRoot" matches an actual key in @generated/schemas/config-schema.json. If the schema key is "StacktapeConfig" or if a narrower "CloudformationResource" definition exists, update definitionName accordingly. Using a wrong or overly broad definitionName causes the ApiReference component to render an empty or irrelevant section, which is a publication blocker.
optionalImprovements: Add one sentence near the second code example (the DeletionPolicy one) clarifying that resources: {} is valid when the stack only uses cloudformationResources — removes a common beginner stumble.

Consolidate the back-to-back Tip + Warning in "Avoiding naming conflicts" into a single Info callout or prose paragraph. The two callouts address the same practical concern (check logical names before choosing yours) and the split adds visual clutter without extra signal.

The intrinsic functions section shows the TypeScript syntax mapping but the accompanying code example does not actually demonstrate a Ref or Fn::GetAtt in use. Consider replacing the alertTopic example (which uses no intrinsic functions) with one that shows a Fn::GetAtt referencing an adjacent raw resource — making the table immediately actionable.

The "Avoiding naming conflicts" section advises running stacktape info:stack --detailed, but this requires a deployed stack. Add a one-sentence note that stacktape compile-template (already mentioned in the Tip below) can be used pre-deployment to inspect generated logical names, so readers know the non-destructive alternative upfront.

The FAQ answer for "Do raw CF resources count toward the Stacktape resource limit?" mentions the 500-resource AWS CloudFormation stack limit. Consider adding a cross-link to AWS docs or a note that this limit covers both Stacktape-managed and raw resources combined — the reader may not realize Stacktape-managed resources also consume slots.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Strong three-way comparison table (Raw CF vs CDK vs Overrides) gives readers a clear decision framework — exactly what this audience needs before reaching for an escape hatch.
The 'When to use' / 'When NOT to use' pair is concrete, opinionated, and correctly cross-references alternatives (overrides, CDK constructs) with reasons rather than just listing them.
Intrinsic functions TypeScript-to-CloudFormation mapping table is highly practical and directly answers the question every reader will have when they first try to write cross-references.
Code examples are realistic (SES email identity, SSM parameter, CloudWatch log group with RetentionInDays) rather than synthetic toy examples.
DeletionPolicy usage is documented with concrete values (Delete, Retain, Snapshot) and a real use case (retaining a log group after stack deletion).

FAQ spans all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (connectTo, resource limits), AWS service mechanics (rollback behavior, CloudFormation limits), and comparison questions (vs CDK, vs overrides).
No style violations detected: TypeScript-only CodeBlocks, no YAML config blocks, no fenced `typescript` blocks with defineConfig, single command per bash block, no marketing language.
The 'Avoiding naming conflicts' section proactively addresses a real production gotcha rather than leaving it for readers to discover on their own.
problems: '<ApiReference definitionName="ConfigRoot" />' — the string `ConfigRoot` does not appear in any of the provided source files (types/stacktape-config/_root.d.ts, __helpers.d.ts, etc.). The source interface is named `StacktapeConfig`. If the generated schema doesn't have a `ConfigRoot` key, the component renders nothing and the page's API reference section is silently empty. The per-resource definitionName convention ends in `Props` (e.g. `WebServiceProps`), which suggests the right key for the raw CF entry type would be something like `CloudformationResource` or `CloudformationResourceProps`, not `ConfigRoot`.

The second CodeBlock example has `resources: {}` — an empty map. CloudFormation stacks require at least some Stacktape-managed resource in practice, and an empty `resources` map looks like a validation error to readers who haven't tried it. The example is simplified to the point of being misleading about real usage.

The page doesn't warn readers about logical-name fragility: if a reader hardcodes a Stacktape-generated CF logical name in a `Ref` or `Fn::GetAtt` cross-reference, that reference can break silently when Stacktape regenerates the name in a future version or config change. This is a real production hazard that the 'Using intrinsic functions' section should acknowledge.

Minor CLI notation inconsistency: the page uses `stacktape info:stack --detailed` but the source JSDoc for `cloudformationResources` uses `stacktape stack-info --detailed`. These may be the same command under different notation styles, but readers searching for the command may get confused if one form doesn't work.
mandatoryFixes: Verify that `definitionName="ConfigRoot"` resolves to an actual entry in the generated config schema. The `StacktapeConfig` root interface in source is named `StacktapeConfig`, not `ConfigRoot`. If the schema uses a different key (e.g. `CloudformationResource`, `CloudformationResourceProps`, or `StacktapeConfig`), update the definitionName to match — an unresolved definitionName renders the API Reference section empty with no error shown to the reader.
optionalImprovements: Replace `resources: {}` in the second CodeBlock with at least one minimal Stacktape-managed resource (e.g. a placeholder LambdaFunction or even a commented intent) so the example resembles a real config. An empty resources map implies raw CF resources can exist standalone, which may confuse readers about Stacktape's deployment model.

Add a one-sentence caution in the 'Using intrinsic functions' section about logical-name stability: Stacktape-generated CF logical names are implementation details that can change across Stacktape versions or config refactors. Where possible, prefer `$ResourceParam()` directives for referencing Stacktape-managed resource attributes rather than hardcoded CF logical names in raw resource definitions.

Confirm and standardize the CLI command: `stacktape info:stack --detailed` vs `stacktape stack-info --detailed`. The source JSDoc uses the latter; the docs route `/cli/info-stack` implies the former. Pick one canonical form and use it consistently in prose and code blocks.

The FAQ answer about the 500-resource CloudFormation limit mentions 'the option to request a limit increase' but doesn't note the practical implication: teams using Stacktape for complex stacks may already be using a significant portion of that budget before they add any raw resources. A sentence flagging this production ceiling would be useful.

The 'Avoiding naming conflicts' section recommends `stacktape compile-template` via a Tip callout. Consider promoting this to inline prose rather than a callout — for readers using raw CF resources in production, previewing the compiled template before every deploy is closer to a required practice than an optional tip.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: The opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and clearly explains the purpose of `cloudformationResources`.
The page uses the required TypeScript class-based Stacktape config examples inside `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/>`, with no hand-authored YAML config.
The early decision sections are useful: `When to use`, `When NOT to use`, and `Choosing an escape hatch` give the reader practical alternatives.
The comparison table helps readers distinguish raw CloudFormation resources, CDK constructs, and overrides quickly.
The FAQ covers Stacktape-specific questions, underlying CloudFormation behavior, and choosing/comparison questions without obvious padding.
problems: The CLI command `stacktape info:stack --detailed` is not grounded in the provided source. The source repeatedly says `stacktape stack-info --detailed`, so the page appears to document the wrong command in multiple places.

The page makes several Stacktape-specific claims that are not grounded in the provided source files, including exact support for `DeletionPolicy`, `DependsOn`, `Condition`, `Metadata`, `CreationPolicy`, and `UpdatePolicy`, and the claim that the raw resource key becomes the CloudFormation logical ID.

The intrinsic-functions section promises an SNS topic subscription referencing a Stacktape-managed resource, but the example only creates a Lambda function and an SNS topic. It does not show a subscription or a cross-reference, so the prose and code disagree.

The `connectTo` FAQ makes a broad negative claim that raw CloudFormation resources cannot use `connectTo` and receive no automatic IAM or environment variables. That may be reasonable, but the provided source only documents `connectTo` for Stacktape-managed resources and does not explicitly define raw CloudFormation behavior in that mechanism.

The FAQ states AWS CloudFormation allows up to 500 resources per stack and that `DeletionPolicy` supports `Delete`, `Retain`, and `Snapshot`; those are AWS facts, but they are not grounded in the provided source files and should be framed explicitly as AWS CloudFormation behavior.

The API reference uses `<ApiReference definitionName="ConfigRoot" />`, but the provided source type is `interface StacktapeConfig`; there is no provided evidence that `ConfigRoot` is a generated schema definition name.
Several paragraphs start with weak antecedents or generic phrasing such as “This example” and “The key,” which is less extractable for AI snippets than explicit entity naming.
mandatoryFixes: Replace every `stacktape info:stack --detailed` command with the source-grounded `stacktape stack-info --detailed`, unless a higher-priority CLI source proves the newer command name.
Fix the intrinsic-functions example so it actually demonstrates the described CloudFormation intrinsic reference, or rewrite the prose to match the example shown.

Verify the API reference definition name against the generated schema. If the generated definition is not `ConfigRoot`, replace `<ApiReference definitionName="ConfigRoot" />` with the exact schema definition name for the root config type.

Qualify unsupported exact CloudFormation-attribute claims. Either cite only what the provided source supports, or phrase attribute examples as standard AWS CloudFormation resource attributes that raw resources can carry because Stacktape merges raw resources into the template as-is.

Narrow the `connectTo` FAQ to the source-grounded fact: `connectTo` is documented on Stacktape resources under `resources`; raw `cloudformationResources` are separate top-level CloudFormation entries. Avoid unsupported claims about every automatic permission/env-var behavior unless source code confirms it.
optionalImprovements: Add a short caution near the first example that raw CloudFormation resources require CloudFormation-level knowledge and are best kept small and self-contained.

Make the naming-conflicts section more source-grounded by saying the source instructs users to inspect existing logical names with `stacktape stack-info --detailed`; avoid adding unsupported claims about a specific “uniqueness validation error.”
Improve extractability by replacing “This example” / “The key” openings with explicit names such as “The SES identity example” and “The `sesEmailIdentity` entry name.”
If pricing data is absent from the provided source, explicitly omit pricing context rather than adding AWS pricing discussion.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the provided Stacktape config source for `cloudformationResources`, but it overstates several behaviors that are not grounded in the supplied types. The highest-impact issues are around CLI command names, raw-resource capabilities, unsupported CloudFormation attribute lists, and claims about integrations that the source only documents for Stacktape-managed resources.

issues.statement: The draft repeatedly tells readers to run `stacktape info:stack --detailed` to inspect generated logical names.
issues.reason: The provided authoritative config type comments for both raw CloudFormation resources and overrides name `stacktape stack-info --detailed`, not `stacktape info:stack --detailed`. No CLI source was provided to confirm the newer command form, so the draft should not present a different command as source-grounded.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the command spelling from the supplied source, or narrow the wording if the docs system has a separate validated CLI command source. For this source set, write `stacktape stack-info --detailed`.

evidence.quote: Use `stacktape stack-info --detailed` to check existing logical names and avoid conflicts.
Find resource logical IDs with `stacktape stack-info --detailed`.

issues.statement: "The supported attributes are `DependsOn`, `Condition`, `DeletionPolicy`, `Metadata`, `CreationPolicy`, and `UpdatePolicy`."
issues.reason: The supplied source confirms only that `cloudformationResources` accepts a `CloudformationResource`, but the definition of `CloudformationResource` is not included. The provided type comments do not enumerate supported CloudFormation resource attributes, so the draft's exact list is not grounded in this source set.
issues.suggestedFix: Phrase this cautiously as standard CloudFormation resource attributes if you keep it, or remove the exact supported-attribute list unless the `CloudformationResource` type is supplied. For example: "Use standard CloudFormation resource syntax for any attributes accepted by AWS CloudFormation."

evidence.quote: cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource };
These are merged into the CloudFormation template as-is.

issues.statement: "Use [`iamRoleStatements`](/configuration/connecting-resources) with the resource's ARN" to grant access to a raw CloudFormation resource.
issues.reason: The source does support `iamRoleStatements` on workloads, but the route `/configuration/connecting-resources` documents `connectTo`, not necessarily raw IAM statements. More importantly, the source only states raw IAM statements are for permissions not covered by `connectTo`; it does not say they are the prescribed way to grant access to raw CloudFormation resources specifically.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the sentence to the source-backed behavior: "For workloads, add raw IAM policy statements with `iamRoleStatements` for permissions not covered by `connectTo`. You must supply the correct AWS actions and resource ARNs yourself."

evidence.quote: Raw IAM policy statements for permissions not covered by `connectTo`.
Use this for accessing AWS services directly (e.g., Rekognition, Textract, Bedrock).

issues.statement: "Built-in resources integrate with `connectTo`, alarms, dev mode, and the Stacktape Console. A raw CloudFormation resource gets none of these."
issues.reason: The provided source supports `connectTo` for Stacktape resources and shows which resource types can have alarms, but it does not establish Console integration or dev mode support for every built-in resource. It also does not explicitly state that raw CloudFormation resources get none of these integrations.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the source-backed contrast narrower: "Raw CloudFormation resources are outside the `resources` map, so they do not participate in Stacktape resource typing or the documented `connectTo` resource list. Prefer built-in resources when Stacktape provides the resource type."

evidence.quote: resources: { [resourceName: string]: StacktapeResourceDefinition };
cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource };

type StpAlarmEnabledResource =
  | StpLambdaFunction
  | StpRelationalDatabase
  | StpHttpApiGateway
  | StpApplicationLoadBalancer
  | StpSqsQueue;

issues.statement: "You can add as many raw CloudFormation resources as the underlying CloudFormation stack supports."
issues.reason: The source says raw CloudFormation resources do not count toward the Stacktape resource limit. It does not state there is no Stacktape-side limit other than CloudFormation's own limits, so "as many as" is broader than the provided source supports.
issues.suggestedFix: Write: "Raw CloudFormation resources do not count toward Stacktape's resource limit. They are still part of the generated CloudFormation template, so AWS CloudFormation stack limits apply."

evidence.quote: Does not count towards your resource limit.
These are merged into the CloudFormation template as-is.

issues.statement: "Raw resources are merged exactly as you define them — no abstraction layer in between."
issues.reason: The source says raw CloudFormation resources are merged into the CloudFormation template as-is, but it does not support the stronger claim that there is no abstraction layer or that every definition is preserved exactly without validation, transformation, or synthesis effects.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the narrower source wording: "Raw resources are merged into the generated CloudFormation template as-is."

evidence.quote: These are merged into the CloudFormation template as-is.

issues.statement: "If a raw CloudFormation resource uses a logical name that collides with a Stacktape-generated one, the deployment will fail with a uniqueness validation error."
issues.reason: The source tells readers to avoid naming conflicts, but it does not describe the failure mode or the exact error class. The deployment-failure claim is plausible CloudFormation behavior, but the specific "uniqueness validation error" wording is not grounded in the supplied source.
issues.suggestedFix: Write cautiously: "Choose names that do not conflict with Stacktape-generated logical IDs; the source explicitly recommends checking existing logical names before deploying."

evidence.quote: Use `stacktape stack-info --detailed` to check existing logical names and avoid conflicts.

issues.statement: The comparison table says CDK constructs are counted toward the Stacktape resource limit and raw CloudFormation resources are not.
issues.reason: The source supports the raw CloudFormation half of the comparison, but it does not state that `AwsCdkConstruct` resources count toward the Stacktape resource limit. Since the comparison is Stacktape-specific and affects escape-hatch choice, the unsupported CDK limit claim should be removed or sourced from the CDK page's type/source.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the raw CloudFormation fact and remove the CDK resource-limit comparison unless the CDK construct source is included. For example: "Raw CloudFormation resources are documented as excluded from the Stacktape resource limit."

evidence.quote: Does not count towards your resource limit.
| AwsCdkConstruct

positiveFindings: The draft correctly uses TypeScript `defineConfig` examples inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` and does not hand-author YAML Stacktape config.
The draft uses the correct `<ApiReference definitionName="ConfigRoot" />` for a page documenting a root-level config property rather than a resource `Props` type.
The core description that `cloudformationResources` is an escape hatch for AWS services without built-in Stacktape resource types is directly supported by `_root.d.ts`.
The statement that raw CloudFormation resources do not count toward the Stacktape resource limit is source-backed.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the provided root config source for `cloudformationResources`, but several Stacktape-specific claims go beyond what the provided sources establish. The highest-impact problems are the API reference definition, unsupported claims about raw resources lacking Stacktape integrations, and unsupported CLI/output behavior around logical IDs.

issues.statement: `<ApiReference definitionName="ConfigRoot" />`
issues.reason: The page metadata is for a resource page about raw CloudFormation resources, but the provided config type is named `StacktapeConfig`, not `ConfigRoot`. The ApiReference discipline requires the definition name to match an actual schema/type name; `ConfigRoot` is not grounded in the provided type files and is likely to render empty or the wrong reference.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the schema/type name that exists for the root config, most likely `<ApiReference definitionName="StacktapeConfig" />`, or remove the API reference only if this page's publishing system has a different documented definition name for root config.

evidence.quote: interface StacktapeConfig {
cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource };

issues.statement: “Run `stacktape info:stack --detailed` to see all CloudFormation logical IDs in your deployed stack” and “Both commands show Stacktape-generated logical names…”
issues.reason: The provided source only says stack info can be used to check existing logical names and avoid conflicts; it does not establish that `info:stack --detailed` shows all CloudFormation logical IDs. The exact command name in the source is also different from the draft. This affects how readers inspect and reference generated resources.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the claim to the sourced behavior: say Stacktape stack info can be used to check existing logical names and avoid conflicts, and link to the CLI reference without claiming it shows all logical IDs unless CLI source confirms that output.

evidence.quote: Use `stacktape stack-info --detailed` to check existing logical names and avoid conflicts.

issues.statement: “A raw CloudFormation resource gets none of these” for `connectTo`, alarms, dev mode, and the Stacktape Console; and the comparison table says raw CloudFormation does not work with `connectTo`, alarms, or observability.
issues.reason: The source supports that `cloudformationResources` are merged into the CloudFormation template and excluded from the resource limit, but it does not explicitly define their integration behavior with alarms, dev mode, Console, or observability. This is likely directionally true, but the provided files do not ground the full negative claim.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more narrowly: built-in Stacktape resources are the documented path for `connectTo`, alarms, dev mode, and Console-aware behavior; raw CloudFormation resources are documented only as template-level additions merged as-is.

evidence.quote: Escape hatch: add raw AWS CloudFormation resources alongside Stacktape-managed ones.
These are merged into the CloudFormation template as-is.
List the names of resources this workload needs to communicate with. Stacktape automatically:

issues.statement: “To grant a Stacktape-managed workload access to a raw CloudFormation resource, use `iamRoleStatements` with the resource's ARN.”
issues.reason: The source supports `iamRoleStatements` as raw IAM policy statements for permissions not covered by `connectTo`, but it does not state that this is specifically how to grant access to raw CloudFormation resources or that the raw resource ARN will be available. The recommendation is plausible, but the wording is broader than the source.
issues.suggestedFix: Say `iamRoleStatements` can add raw IAM policy statements for permissions not covered by `connectTo`; if the raw AWS resource exposes an ARN, use the ARN or an intrinsic function in the IAM policy as appropriate.

evidence.quote: Raw IAM policy statements for permissions not covered by `connectTo`.
Added as a separate policy alongside auto-generated permissions. Use this for accessing AWS services directly (e.g., Rekognition, Textract, Bedrock).

issues.statement: “You can set `DeletionPolicy`, `DependsOn`, `Condition`, `Metadata`, `CreationPolicy`, and `UpdatePolicy` directly” and “The supported attributes are…”
issues.reason: The provided source references `CloudformationResource` but does not include the `CloudformationResource` type definition or enumerate supported top-level CloudFormation attributes. Standard CloudFormation supports these concepts, but the exact Stacktape config type support is not grounded in the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Avoid the exhaustive supported-attributes claim unless the `CloudformationResource` type is provided. Use cautious wording such as “raw resources follow CloudFormation resource syntax, including standard resource attributes accepted by the CloudFormation resource schema.”

evidence.quote: cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource };
These are merged into the CloudFormation template as-is.

issues.statement: “The key `sesEmailIdentity` becomes the CloudFormation logical ID for the resource.”
issues.reason: The source says `cloudformationResources` is a map keyed by resource name and that raw resources are merged into the CloudFormation template, but it does not explicitly state that the map key is used unchanged as the CloudFormation logical ID. This is an important behavior for references and naming conflicts.
issues.suggestedFix: Phrase cautiously: “The map key is the Stacktape config name for the raw resource; use stack info or the compiled template to confirm the final CloudFormation logical ID before referencing it.”

evidence.quote: cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource };
Use `stacktape stack-info --detailed` to check existing logical names and avoid conflicts.

issues.statement: The “Using intrinsic functions” example says it creates an SNS topic subscription that references a Stacktape-managed resource, but the code only creates an SNS topic and does not include a subscription or a cross-reference.
issues.reason: This is internally inconsistent and likely confuses readers even though the broader claim that intrinsic functions can be represented as objects is reasonable CloudFormation usage.
issues.suggestedFix: Either change the prose to say the example creates an SNS topic, or update the example to include an actual subscription and intrinsic reference.

issues.statement: “Stacktape offers 30+ built-in resource types.”
issues.reason: The provided `StacktapeResourceDefinition` union contains many resource definitions, but the exact public count is ambiguous because some entries are helper/definition variants or may not correspond one-to-one with documented resource types. The claim is probably true, but the numeric marketing-style count is unnecessary and not directly sourced.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with “Stacktape offers many built-in resource types” or link the resource catalog without a numeric count.

positiveFindings: The draft correctly uses TypeScript `defineConfig` examples inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` and does not hand-author YAML Stacktape config.

The first paragraph accurately reflects the sourced core behavior: `cloudformationResources` adds raw CloudFormation resources, merges them into the template as-is, and does not count toward the resource limit.
The page correctly treats raw CloudFormation as an escape hatch and recommends built-in Stacktape resources when available, which matches the source framing.
The draft includes exactly one API reference component, though the definition name needs correction.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the main escape-hatch decision well, but it has one material code-example problem and a few Stacktape-specific claims that are not supported by the provided ConfigRoot source. I did not flag omissions of unrelated ConfigRoot properties because this page is scoped to `cloudformationResources` and includes the `ConfigRoot` API reference.

issues.statement: In the `Using intrinsic functions` section, the example is introduced as showing how to reference a Stacktape-managed resource by CloudFormation logical name, but the code only creates a `LambdaFunction` and a raw SNS topic. It does not use `Ref`, `Fn::GetAtt`, `Fn::Sub`, or any reference to the Lambda logical ID.
issues.reason: The example is not demonstrative of the section topic. Readers looking for intrinsic-function usage get a generic raw-resource example instead of a working intrinsic-function example.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace the example with a raw CloudFormation resource that actually uses an intrinsic function, such as an SNS subscription or Lambda permission using `{ Ref: 'SomeLogicalId' }` or `{ 'Fn::GetAtt': ['SomeLogicalId', 'Arn'] }`. Keep any Stacktape-generated logical-ID references narrow and caveated.

evidence.quote: For example, to create an SNS topic subscription that references a Stacktape-managed resource by its CloudFormation logical name...

evidence.file: Draft page: Using intrinsic functions code
evidence.quote: cloudformationResources: { alertTopic: { Type: 'AWS::SNS::Topic', Properties: { TopicName: 'deployment-alerts', DisplayName: 'Deployment Alerts' } } }

issues.statement: “The supported attributes are `DependsOn`, `Condition`, `DeletionPolicy`, `Metadata`, `CreationPolicy`, and `UpdatePolicy`.”
issues.reason: The provided source only exposes `cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource }` and says raw CloudFormation resources are merged as-is. The `CloudformationResource` shape is not included in the provided source files, so the specific allowed attribute list is not grounded here.
issues.suggestedFix: Either cite/source the `CloudformationResource` type in the source set, or narrow the prose to say that raw resources follow CloudFormation resource syntax and can include CloudFormation resource attributes supported by that syntax.

evidence.quote: cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource };

issues.statement: “A raw CloudFormation resource gets none of these” for `connectTo`, alarms, dev mode, and Stacktape Console integration.
issues.reason: The source says `cloudformationResources` are merged into the CloudFormation template as-is and do not count toward the resource limit. It does not explicitly define which Stacktape features raw CloudFormation resources never integrate with.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the decision guidance but make it narrower: “Raw CloudFormation resources are not Stacktape resource definitions in the `resources` map, so do not expect the same typed resource configuration surface as built-in resources.” Link to built-in resources for features like `connectTo`.

evidence.quote: resources: { [resourceName: string]: StacktapeResourceDefinition };
cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource };

issues.statement: “Works with connectTo: false”, “Works with alarms and observability: false”, and “CDK Constructs … Counted toward resource limit: true” in the escape-hatch comparison table.
issues.reason: The provided source grounds that `cloudformationResources` do not count toward the resource limit, but it does not ground the comparison-table claims for CDK constructs, alarms, observability, or `connectTo` behavior across all escape hatches.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove unsupported boolean comparison rows or soften them into prose limited to the sourced distinction: raw CloudFormation entries are separate from typed Stacktape resources and are excluded from the resource limit. Keep detailed comparisons only where the relevant source files are included.

evidence.quote: Does not count towards your resource limit.
| AwsCdkConstruct

issues.statement: “Use `iamRoleStatements` with the resource's ARN” to grant access to a raw CloudFormation resource.
issues.reason: `iamRoleStatements` is documented on workload access props, but the provided source does not show a supported way to obtain the ARN of a raw CloudFormation resource or prove that every raw resource has an ARN suitable for this pattern.
issues.suggestedFix: Say that workloads can be granted permissions with `iamRoleStatements` when you know the target ARN or can construct/reference it through CloudFormation, and avoid implying every raw resource exposes an ARN automatically through Stacktape.

evidence.quote: iamRoleStatements?: StpIamRoleStatement[];

positiveFindings: The draft uses `defineConfig` and class-based Stacktape configuration examples inside `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a visible `label: 'TypeScript'` tab, and it does not hand-author YAML Stacktape config.
The page includes exactly one `<ApiReference definitionName="ConfigRoot" />`, matching the provided page metadata.
The page clearly distinguishes raw CloudFormation from built-in `resources`, which is grounded by the separate `resources` and `cloudformationResources` properties in `ConfigRoot`.
The main `cloudformationResources` API surface is documented early, and the draft includes practical guidance on choosing raw CloudFormation versus other escape hatches.

verifierResults.summary: Deterministic check of <CodeBlock intellisense> Stacktape config examples and generated MDX component usage.

issues.statement: <CodeBlock intellisense> #2 (TypeScript): cannot generate YAML tab from TypeScript source.
issues.reason: The TypeScript example parsed and type-checked, but the docs YAML converter could not derive a YAML tab. This may be a docs-converter coverage gap for valid class-based config.
issues.suggestedFix: If the example is valid Stacktape TypeScript, extend the YAML converter for this class/property shape. Otherwise simplify the example to a converter-supported class-based defineConfig shape.

issues.statement: <ApiReference definitionName="ConfigRoot" /> references an unknown schema definition.
issues.reason: ConfigRoot is not present in @generated/schemas/config-schema.json definitions.
issues.suggestedFix: Use a definitionName that exists in @generated/schemas/config-schema.json.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Strong, self-contained opening paragraph that directly answers 'what is this and why does it matter' in ~60 words — good AEO hygiene.
Opinionated 'When to use' / 'When NOT to use' structure with concrete, clearly differentiated guidance (overrides vs. raw CF vs. CDK), including an explicit recommendation at the end.
Realistic, copyable examples across multiple sections (SES email identity, SNS topic, SSM parameter, SQS DLQ) — each illustrates a genuinely common use case.
The intrinsic-function syntax-mapping table (CloudFormation syntax → TypeScript object form) is highly practical and exactly what a first-timer needs when they hit their first `Ref`.
The `<FeatureComparisonTable>` comparing all three escape hatches is a strong decision-support tool and very scannable.
Comprehensive FAQ with 10 Q&A pairs that correctly span all three required categories: Stacktape-specific, underlying-AWS-service, and choosing/comparison questions.
All Stacktape config examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} />` with `label: 'TypeScript'` — no forbidden YAML or fenced-TypeScript violations.
Shell commands are properly split into one-per-block with prose introductions — no multi-command copy-button trap.
problems: MANDATORY STYLE VIOLATION — `<ApiReference definitionName="default" />` uses 'default' which is not a valid schema definition name. The Stacktape config types end in `Props` or use their declared type name; the value type for `cloudformationResources` entries appears to be `CloudformationResource` in the source. A wrong `definitionName` renders an empty API reference section, removing the exhaustive-options discovery path for readers. This triggers the '`<ApiReference>` whose `definitionName` does not match any generated config-schema definition' rule and must penalize `completeness` by at least 2.

CLI command factual error: the page repeatedly uses `stacktape stack-info --detailed` but Stacktape's consistent colon-separated CLI pattern (`debug:logs`, `info:stacks`, `info:operations`, `info:whoami` as seen in the CLI docs file names) indicates the correct command is `stacktape info:stack --detailed`. Using the wrong command syntax will cause a 'command not found' error for readers who copy it.
The broken `<ApiReference definitionName="default" />` is both a factual error and a style violation — it should appear in both `problems` and `mandatoryFixes`.
mandatoryFixes: `<ApiReference definitionName="default" />` — 'default' does not match any TypeScript type in the provided source. The value type for entries in `cloudformationResources` is `CloudformationResource` (from `cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource }` in `_root.d.ts`). Look up the exact schema key used for `CloudformationResource` in `@generated/schemas/config-schema.json` and replace 'default' with that key (e.g., `"CloudformationResource"` or its `Props`-suffixed variant if applicable). A wrong `definitionName` renders an empty section and breaks the reader's path to exhaustive property discovery. Penalizes `completeness` by 2.

Replace every instance of `stacktape stack-info --detailed` with `stacktape info:stack --detailed` (and update surrounding prose accordingly). Based on Stacktape's consistent `group:command` pattern — `debug:logs`, `info:stacks`, `info:operations`, `info:whoami` — the file `info-stack.mdx` maps to the CLI command `info:stack`, not `stack-info`. Readers who copy the current text will get a command-not-found error.
optionalImprovements: Verify whether `stacktape compile-template` is the correct command syntax or whether it should be `stacktape compile:template` — the colon-vs-hyphen pattern needs a check against the CLI source (`src/commands/compile-template/index.ts`).

The 'Resource attributes' section lists all eight supported attributes but doesn't signal which are commonly used vs. rarely needed. A one-sentence note like 'Most teams only need `Type`, `Properties`, and `DeletionPolicy`; `CreationPolicy` and `Metadata` are rarely used' would help readers focus.

Consider adding a brief note about `cloudformationResources` behavior during `stacktape dev` mode — are raw CF resources deployed as-is, skipped, or unavailable during local development? This is a common first-timer question that is currently unanswered.

The 'Basic example' section could note explicitly that the Stacktape config map key (e.g., `sesEmailIdentity`) becomes the CloudFormation logical ID — the current wording says to 'confirm the final CloudFormation logical ID' with `compile-template`, which implies it might differ when it actually shouldn't for raw resources.

The FAQ answer for DependsOn between raw and Stacktape-managed resources could be slightly more concrete — mention that Stacktape logical names often include a hash or prefix derived from the resource name, which is why `compile-template` verification matters before each major upgrade.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Clear, opinionated when-to-use / when-not-to-use sections grounded in actual source behavior (connectTo, overrides, CDK tradeoffs) — gives readers a real decision, not just feature description.

The three-way escape-hatch comparison table (Raw CF vs CDK vs Overrides) is the page's strongest asset: covers all alternatives, contrasts the right dimensions, ends with a prioritized recommendation.
IAM grants section correctly identifies that raw CF resources are outside the connectTo graph and shows iamRoleStatements + Fn::GetAtt as the alternative — critical gotcha for production use.
Intrinsic functions reference table (CF syntax → TypeScript object form) is a high-signal, scannable snippet that developers will reach for repeatedly.
All Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs=[TypeScript]> correctly; no fenced YAML blocks, no StacktapeConfig typed style.
Shell commands are correctly split into separate fenced blocks (one command each), with no explanatory # comments inside blocks.

FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific (connectTo, resource limit, DeletionPolicy), AWS-service questions (rollback behavior, CF pricing model), and comparison questions (raw CF vs CDK, raw CF vs overrides).
problems: MANDATORY: `<ApiReference definitionName="default" />` — 'default' is not a valid schema definition name. The correct value must match the generated config-schema key for the CloudformationResource type (e.g. 'CloudformationResource'). This will render an empty or broken API reference section, the only API reference on the page. Penalizes completeness by at least 2.

The FeatureComparisonTable row 'Counted toward resource limit — CDK Constructs: true' is asserted without explicit source grounding in the provided files. The source confirms cloudformationResources 'Does not count towards your resource limit' for raw CF, but the same explicit statement for CDK constructs (AwsCdkConstruct being in resources) is not stated in the provided source.

The 'Resource attributes' section enumerates specific CloudFormation resource attributes (Condition, CreationPolicy, UpdatePolicy, etc.) as supported. The CloudformationResource type definition is not included in the provided source files, so this enumeration is not source-grounded from the inline files. These are accurate AWS CloudFormation facts, but the claim is presented as a Stacktape-specific enumeration.
mandatoryFixes: Replace `<ApiReference definitionName="default" />` with the correct definitionName matching the CloudformationResource type in the generated config-schema. 'default' is not a valid schema entry name and will render an empty API reference section. Per rules: wrong definitionName penalizes completeness by at least 2 points. Look up the exact key from `@generated/schemas/config-schema.json` — it is likely 'CloudformationResource' or 'CloudformationResourceProps'.
optionalImprovements: Qualify the CDK 'Counted toward resource limit: true' cell in the comparison table with a brief source citation or link to the CDK constructs page, since the provided source only explicitly states the negative (raw CF does not count) and is silent on CDK.

The 'Resource attributes' section could open with a note that these are standard CloudFormation resource attributes and link to the AWS CloudFormation resource syntax docs rather than implying Stacktape defines which attributes are valid — this would reduce any source-grounding exposure.

The page links to '/cli/info-stack' for the `stacktape stack-info --detailed` command in the FAQ but not in the 'Avoiding naming conflicts' prose section — add a link there too for consistency with the cross-page linking rule.

Consider adding a sentence in the 'Avoiding naming conflicts' section noting that Stacktape's stack-level tags (projectName, stage, stackName) are automatically applied to CF resources that support tagging, which can help with cost attribution even for raw resources.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, concrete, and grounded in the `cloudformationResources` source comment.
The page uses TypeScript class-based Stacktape config examples inside `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/>` and avoids hand-authored Stacktape YAML.
The decision framing is useful: raw CloudFormation vs built-in resources, overrides, and CDK constructs is the right comparison for this escape hatch.
The FAQ covers Stacktape-specific, AWS behavior, and choosing/comparison questions without padding.
problems: Several Stacktape-specific claims are broader than the provided source supports. The source says raw CloudFormation resources are merged as-is, do not count toward the resource limit, and can be inspected with `stacktape stack-info --detailed`; it does not prove the accepted CloudFormation attribute list, Console integration behavior, dev mode behavior, or exact logical-ID stability semantics.

The `Resource attributes` section states a precise supported attribute list (`Type`, `Properties`, `DependsOn`, `Condition`, `DeletionPolicy`, `Metadata`, `CreationPolicy`, `UpdatePolicy`) without the `CloudformationResource` type in the provided source. That should be narrowed or explicitly tied to standard CloudFormation template attributes rather than Stacktape validation support.

The `Using intrinsic functions` example says it demonstrates `Fn::GetAtt` wiring up a Lambda permission, but the code block does not contain `Fn::GetAtt` or a Lambda permission. This is a concrete example/prose mismatch.

The `Granting workload access` example sets `Resource` to an intrinsic-function object, but the provided `StpIamRoleStatement.Resource` type is `string[]`. Unless another source permits intrinsic functions there, the example appears type-invalid against the supplied type definition.

The page repeatedly recommends `stacktape compile-template` for discovering generated logical names, but the provided root source only says to use `stacktape stack-info --detailed` for existing logical names. `compile-template` may be valid CLI behavior, but it is not grounded in the supplied source list.

The FAQ includes unsupported specifics such as CloudFormation's 500-resource stack limit and `disableAutoRollback` behavior. Those may be AWS/Stacktape facts elsewhere, but they are not grounded in the provided files for this review.

The first paragraph and comparison table say raw CloudFormation resources do not count toward the Stacktape resource limit, which is sourced, but the FAQ extends that into 'as many raw resources as the underlying CloudFormation stack supports' plus a numeric AWS limit; that needs a source or softer wording.

Some example tunables are insufficiently anchored: `MessageRetentionPeriod: 1209600`, `RetentionInDays: 365`, `LogGroupName: '/custom/audit-trail'`, and wildcard SES IAM `Resource: ['*']` deserve brief context so they do not read as defaults or required values.
mandatoryFixes: Fix the intrinsic-functions section so the prose matches the code: either add a real `Fn::GetAtt`/Lambda permission example that type-checks, or remove the claim that the example demonstrates `Fn::GetAtt` and Lambda permission wiring.
Correct the `iamRoleStatements.Resource` example to match the provided `StpIamRoleStatement.Resource: string[]` type, or provide a grounded source proving intrinsic functions are accepted there.

Remove or narrow unsupported Stacktape-specific claims about Console integration, dev mode integration, `compile-template` logical-name discovery, accepted raw-resource attributes, and exact rollback behavior unless those claims are backed by additional source files.
Replace the precise 'supported attributes are...' wording with cautious CloudFormation-oriented wording unless the `CloudformationResource` type source is available.
Add short prose anchors for non-obvious tunable values used in examples, especially `MessageRetentionPeriod: 1209600`, `RetentionInDays: 365`, and `Resource: ['*']`.
optionalImprovements: Link the first mention of built-in resource types to the resource catalog in the opening or first decision section, not only inside a later bullet.
Consider changing `Resource attributes` to `CloudFormation syntax` for a shorter, more reader-oriented H2.
Add a brief warning near the first example that raw CloudFormation resources require AWS-level permissions, lifecycle awareness, and service-specific validation from CloudFormation.
If `compile-template` is retained after sourcing, clarify the distinction: `stack-info --detailed` inspects deployed stacks; `compile-template` previews the generated template before deploy.

verifierResults.summary: The draft gets the core purpose of `cloudformationResources` right, including its top-level placement, merge-as-is behavior, and exclusion from the Stacktape resource limit. The main blockers are over-specific claims that are not grounded in the provided types and one TypeScript config example that contradicts the `iamRoleStatements` type.

issues.statement: In the “Granting workload access to raw resources” example, `iamRoleStatements.Resource` is set to `[{ 'Fn::GetAtt': ['customDeadLetterQueue', 'Arn'] }]`, and the prose says to “Use intrinsic functions like `Fn::GetAtt` to reference the raw resource's ARN dynamically rather than hardcoding it.”
issues.reason: The provided Stacktape config type for IAM statements only allows `Resource: string[]`. An intrinsic-function object inside the `Resource` array is not supported by the shown type and would likely fail TypeScript validation for the documented config style.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the example to use a string resource value that matches `StpIamRoleStatement.Resource`, or avoid showing dynamic IAM ARNs through `iamRoleStatements`. If dynamic ARN wiring is needed, document it cautiously as an advanced CloudFormation/IAM-policy pattern only if the relevant source type supports it.

issues.statement: “The supported attributes are `Type` (required), `Properties`, `DependsOn`, `Condition`, `DeletionPolicy`, `Metadata`, `CreationPolicy`, and `UpdatePolicy`.”
issues.reason: The provided source confirms a `cloudformationResources` map whose values are `CloudformationResource`, but it does not include the `CloudformationResource` definition or its exact required/optional attributes. The exact supported-attribute list is therefore not grounded in the supplied source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to say raw entries use CloudFormation resource objects and link to AWS CloudFormation resource syntax, or only enumerate attributes if the `CloudformationResource` type definition is included and confirms them.

evidence.quote: cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource };

issues.statement: “The `iamRoleStatements` property is available on all Stacktape compute resources ([Lambda functions], [web services], [worker services], [batch jobs], and [multi-container workloads]).”
issues.reason: The provided source shows `iamRoleStatements` on `ResourceAccessProps`, but it does not prove that every compute resource type listed by Stacktape inherits that property. The source also lists additional workload types, including private services and edge Lambda functions, so the phrase “all Stacktape compute resources” is broader than the evidence provided.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more cautiously, for example: “Workload resources that expose `iamRoleStatements` can use raw IAM policy statements; check the API reference for the specific workload type.”

evidence.quote:
type StpWorkloadDefinition =
  | StpLambdaFunction
  | StpContainerWorkload
  | StpBatchJob
  | StpWebService
  | StpPrivateService
  | StpWorkerService
  | StpEdgeLambdaFunction;

interface ResourceAccessProps {
  /**
   * #### Give this resource access to other resources in your stack.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * List the names of resources this workload needs to communicate with. Stacktape automatically:
   * - **Grants IAM permissions** (e.g., S3 read/write, SQS send/receive)
   * - **Opens network access** (security group rules for databases, Redis)
   * - **Injects environment variables** with connection details: `STP_[RESOURCE_NAME]_[PARAM]`
iamRoleStatements?: StpIamRoleStatement[];

issues.statement: “Built-in resources defined in the `resources` map are the documented path for … [Stacktape Console] integration.”
issues.reason: The supplied source files describe the `resources` map and `cloudformationResources`, but they do not include Console source or any statement about how raw CloudFormation resources appear or do not appear in Stacktape Console. The claim may be true, but it is not grounded in the provided source set.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the Console integration claim or narrow it to the source-backed distinction: raw CloudFormation resources sit outside the typed `resources` map and outside the documented `connectTo` resource graph.

evidence.quote: resources: { [resourceName: string]: StacktapeResourceDefinition };
cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource };

issues.statement: The “Using intrinsic functions” prose says the example uses `Fn::GetAtt` “to wire up a Lambda permission,” but the shown code does not include `Fn::GetAtt` or any Lambda permission resource.
issues.reason: The example demonstrates `Ref` and `DependsOn`, but not the second pattern described in the prose. This makes the section internally inconsistent even if intrinsic functions are otherwise a valid CloudFormation concept.
issues.suggestedFix: Either update the prose to describe only the `Ref` example, or add a source-valid example that actually includes `Fn::GetAtt` and a Lambda permission.

issues.statement: The draft repeatedly says `stacktape compile-template` can be used to discover final logical IDs and preview the full generated template.
issues.reason: The provided source for `cloudformationResources` specifically mentions `stacktape stack-info --detailed` for checking logical names and avoiding conflicts. The CLI implementation for `compile-template` is not included in the supplied sources, so the stronger behavior claim is not verifiable from this source set.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep `stacktape stack-info --detailed` as the source-grounded command. Mention `compile-template` only if the CLI source or generated CLI docs confirm the exact behavior.

positiveFindings: The opening paragraph is grounded in `_root.d.ts`: `cloudformationResources` is top-level, is an escape hatch for raw AWS CloudFormation resources, merges into the template as-is, and does not count toward the Stacktape resource limit.
All Stacktape config examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and avoid hand-authored YAML Stacktape configuration.
The page correctly uses `/cli/debug-logs` nowhere and does not invent `stacktape logs` in its own prose.
The “When to use” and “When NOT to use” sections are peer H2 sections, not incorrectly nested.
The draft includes exactly one `<ApiReference>` component and avoids removed `<PropertiesTable>` usage.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the provided root config source for the existence and purpose of `cloudformationResources`, but it makes several unsupported Stacktape-specific claims around CloudFormation resource shape, IAM typing, command behavior, and API reference metadata. The highest-impact issue is an invalid-looking config example: `iamRoleStatements.Resource` is typed as `string[]`, but the draft uses an intrinsic object in that array.

issues.statement: The `Granting workload access to raw resources` example sets `iamRoleStatements.Resource: [{ 'Fn::GetAtt': ['customDeadLetterQueue', 'Arn'] }]`.
issues.reason: The provided Stacktape type for IAM role statements requires `Resource` to be `string[]`. The example uses an intrinsic-function object inside the array, which is not supported by the visible type definition and would mislead readers into writing a config shape that does not match the source types.
issues.suggestedFix: Use a string ARN pattern, or rewrite the example to avoid claiming that `iamRoleStatements.Resource` accepts intrinsic function objects unless a higher-priority source explicitly supports it.

issues.statement: The draft lists `Type`, `Properties`, `DependsOn`, `Condition`, `DeletionPolicy`, `Metadata`, `CreationPolicy`, and `UpdatePolicy` as the supported attributes for raw CloudFormation resources.
issues.reason: The provided source only says `cloudformationResources` is a map of `CloudformationResource`; it does not include the `CloudformationResource` definition or enumerate supported attributes. The draft presents a closed supported-attributes list that is not grounded in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to say raw resources are configured as CloudFormation resource objects and show only attributes demonstrated in examples, or cite a source that defines `CloudformationResource` with these attributes.

evidence.quote: cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource };

issues.statement: `<ApiReference definitionName="default" />` is used for the page API reference.
issues.reason: The visible source defines `StacktapeConfig` and the `cloudformationResources` property, but there is no provided metadata typeName or source definition named `default`. The API reference discipline requires the definition name to match the page typeName when present, or the canonical type from source when missing. `default` is not grounded in the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Use a source-backed definition name, likely `StacktapeConfig` if the generated schema exposes the root config by that name, or remove/replace the API reference only if this page intentionally has no schema-backed definition.

issues.statement: Raw CloudFormation resources are outside Stacktape's `connectTo` resource graph and the `connectTo` mechanism works only with Stacktape-managed resources defined in `resources`.
issues.reason: The source supports that `resources` entries are `StacktapeResourceDefinition` and that `cloudformationResources` are separate, but it does not explicitly define a `connectTo` resource graph or state that `connectTo` cannot reference raw CloudFormation resources. This is likely true, but the draft states it as a hard behavior without direct source support.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more cautiously: `cloudformationResources` are separate from the typed `resources` map, so the documented `connectTo` examples and injected variables apply to Stacktape resources, not raw CloudFormation entries.

evidence.quote: resources: { [resourceName: string]: StacktapeResourceDefinition };
cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource };
connectTo?: string[];

issues.statement: The draft says `iamRoleStatements` is available on all Stacktape compute resources, listing Lambda functions, web services, worker services, batch jobs, and multi-container workloads.
issues.reason: The provided source shows `iamRoleStatements` on `ResourceAccessProps`, and `SimpleServiceContainer` extends `ResourceAccessProps`, but the provided excerpts do not show LambdaFunction, BatchJob, WebService, WorkerService, or MultiContainerWorkload prop inheritance. The broad all-compute-resources claim is not fully grounded by the provided files.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the sentence to `Stacktape workloads that expose ResourceAccessProps support iamRoleStatements`, or cite the specific workload type definitions that prove each listed resource supports it.

issues.statement: The `When to use` section names SES email identities, Cognito Identity Pools, CloudWatch Dashboards, AWS Glue jobs, and AppSync APIs as common examples of AWS services without a dedicated Stacktape abstraction.
issues.reason: The source lists the Stacktape built-in resource union, and those specific AWS services are not present, but the source does not support the claim that these are common examples. The page can say they are examples of CloudFormation resource types not visible in the built-in resource union, but not that many teams commonly need them.
issues.suggestedFix: Change `Common examples include... services that many teams need` to `Examples not shown in the built-in resource union include...` or remove the popularity claim.

issues.statement: The comparison table says CDK constructs count toward the Stacktape resource limit, while raw CloudFormation resources do not.
issues.reason: The source explicitly says `cloudformationResources` do not count toward the resource limit, but it does not define how `AwsCdkConstruct` is counted. Because `AwsCdkConstruct` appears in the `StacktapeResourceDefinition` union, the claim is plausible, but the resource-limit counting behavior for CDK constructs is not directly stated.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the raw CloudFormation row as `false`, but avoid asserting CDK construct resource-limit behavior unless a CDK construct source file or resource-limit source explicitly supports it.

evidence.quote: Does not count towards your resource limit.
| AwsCdkConstruct

issues.statement: The draft says `stacktape compile-template` previews the full generated CloudFormation template before deploying.
issues.reason: The route `/cli/compile-template` exists in the documentation index, but the provided source files do not include CLI command source proving what the command outputs or whether `preview` is the right behavior. The claim may be correct, but it is not verifiable from the supplied source excerpts.
issues.suggestedFix: Either cite CLI source for `compile-template`, or narrow the wording to `Use stacktape compile-template to inspect the generated template` if that is the documented command purpose elsewhere.

positiveFindings: The opening paragraph correctly grounds `cloudformationResources` as a top-level Stacktape config property that adds raw CloudFormation resources, merges them into the template as-is, and does not count toward the resource limit.
The draft avoids forbidden hand-authored Stacktape YAML examples and uses `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a visible `TypeScript` tab for Stacktape config examples.
The `When to use` and `When NOT to use` sections are peer H2 sections, so the page does not violate the heading hierarchy rule.
The draft correctly links only to routes that appear in the provided documentation navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: The page is broadly strong: it documents the escape-hatch purpose, when to use it, when not to use it, intrinsic functions, IAM access, naming risks, and alternatives. The main publication risk is the API reference definition name mismatch; the rest of the issues are mostly unsupported or over-specific claims relative to the provided source set.

issues.statement: <ApiReference definitionName="default" />
issues.reason: The page metadata says the primary typeName is ConfigRoot, and the instructions require the ApiReference definitionName to match the page typeName exactly. Using default will likely render the wrong or empty API reference.
issues.suggestedFix: Change the component to <ApiReference definitionName="ConfigRoot" />.

evidence.quote: Primary typeName: ConfigRoot
The <ApiReference definitionName="..." /> value MUST match the page's typeName from metadata exactly.

issues.statement: The supported attributes are Type (required), Properties, DependsOn, Condition, DeletionPolicy, Metadata, CreationPolicy, and UpdatePolicy.
issues.reason: The provided source only shows cloudformationResources as a map of CloudformationResource values. It does not include the CloudformationResource definition, so this exact attribute allow-list is not grounded in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Either cite the CloudformationResource type definition if available in the real source set, or narrow the wording to the source-grounded minimum: each entry is a CloudformationResource and raw resources are merged into the CloudFormation template as-is. If keeping the attribute list, ensure the missing CloudformationResource type source is included.

evidence.quote: cloudformationResources?: { [resourceName: string]: CloudformationResource };

issues.statement: To inspect the logical names Stacktape has assigned, use either of these commands: stacktape stack-info --detailed; stacktape compile-template
issues.reason: The source JSDoc supports stacktape stack-info --detailed for inspecting logical names, and the navigation index supports compile-template as a CLI page, but the provided source files do not state that compile-template is specifically a supported way to inspect assigned logical names. This may be true, but it is not grounded in the provided source set.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep stacktape stack-info --detailed as the source-grounded command, and phrase compile-template more cautiously unless CLI source is included: for example, 'You can also preview the generated template with stacktape compile-template before deploying.'

evidence.quote: Use `stacktape stack-info --detailed` to check existing logical names and avoid conflicts.

The all-compute-resources claim is broader than the provided evidence.
